title: What is AI?
subtitle: Difference between Explicit Programming and Machine Learning!
overview: Students discover what Artificial Intelligence really means — and how it differs from regular programming. Through fun comparisons and activities, they learn that traditional (explicit) programming follows strict rules written by humans, while Machine Learning lets computers learn patterns from data on their own — just like how we learn from experience!

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — AI concept diagrams, ML vs programming flowcharts, activity photos.

keyTopics: What is Intelligence? — Ability to learn, reason, and solve problems
What is Artificial Intelligence? — Making machines mimic human intelligence
Explicit Programming — Humans write exact rules: IF this → DO that (calculator, traffic light)
Limitations of Explicit Programming — Can't handle new situations not covered by rules
Machine Learning — Computer learns rules automatically from lots of examples (data)
Training vs Prediction — ML first trains on data, then predicts on new inputs
Supervised Learning — Learning from labelled examples (cat/dog photos with labels)
Real-world AI examples — Face recognition, spam filters, YouTube recommendations, voice assistants
AI vs ML vs Deep Learning — AI is the big idea; ML is one approach; Deep Learning is a powerful ML technique
Bias in AI — If training data is unfair, the AI learns to be unfair too

activity.title: Be the Algorithm! — Card Sorting Classifier
activity.description: Students experience the difference between explicit programming and machine learning by acting as both a rule-based classifier and a learning classifier.
parts: Set of picture cards (animals, fruits, vehicles — printed or drawn)
steps: Round 1 — Explicit Programming: Teacher gives written IF-THEN rules (e.g. IF has 4 legs AND fur → Cat). Students must sort cards using ONLY the written rules. Discuss: what happens with a photo the rules don't cover?

Round 2 — Machine Learning: No rules given! Show students 10 labelled example cards. They study the patterns themselves. Now give 5 new unlabelled cards — can they classify correctly from what they learned?
Discuss: Which round was easier to adapt to new examples? Which needed more preparation?
Online Activity: Open Google Teachable Machine. Train it with webcam photos of two hand gestures. Test how well it predicts — this is real ML running in the browser!
Reflection: Where do you see AI in your daily life? Make a list on the board.
Challenge: What could go wrong if the training data only shows one type of person or object? Discuss AI bias!

comparisonTable.title: Explicit Programming vs Machine Learning
rows: Only if a rule was written for it
Can generalise to new, unseen inputs
Yes — needs many examples to learn from
Yes — rules are visible and logical
Hard — the model is often a 'black box'
Complex problems with patterns in data

options: Automated Internet
Artificial Intelligence
Advanced Input
Arithmetic Integration
quiz.explanation: AI = Artificial Intelligence — making machines that can perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, like recognising faces or understanding speech.

quiz.q: In explicit programming, who writes the rules the computer follows?
options: The computer learns them from data
The user teaches them by example
The human programmer writes every rule
Rules are downloaded from the internet
quiz.explanation: Explicit Programming = human-written rules. IF this happens → DO that. Every situation must be covered by a rule the programmer wrote.

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of explicit programming?
options: YouTube recommending videos you like
A calculator that adds two numbers
A spam filter learning from emails
Face unlock on a phone
quiz.explanation: A calculator follows exact rules: press 2 + 3, always get 5. No learning involved — the programmer wrote every operation!

options: Teaching a robot to walk using remote control
Programming a computer with thousands of IF-THEN rules
Letting a computer find patterns and learn rules automatically from data
Building a machine that repairs itself
quiz.explanation: Machine Learning = the computer studies lots of examples (data) and figures out the rules itself — without a human writing every rule!

options: Exercising the computer's processor
Feeding the model many labelled examples so it can learn patterns
Writing rules line by line in code
Connecting the computer to the internet
quiz.explanation: Training = showing the model thousands of examples with correct answers, so it learns to recognise patterns and make predictions on new data.

quiz.q: Which of these is a real-world example of Machine Learning?
options: A traffic light switching green after 60 seconds
An ATM dispensing cash when correct PIN is entered
Gmail automatically filtering spam emails
A calculator dividing two numbers
quiz.explanation: Gmail's spam filter uses ML — it learned from millions of spam examples what spam looks like, and keeps learning from new emails!

quiz.q: What is the key difference between explicit programming and ML in terms of rules?
options: Explicit programming has no rules; ML has many
In explicit programming humans write rules; in ML the computer learns rules from data
Both work exactly the same way
ML uses rules written in binary; explicit uses English
quiz.explanation: The core difference: Explicit → human writes rules. ML → machine learns rules from data. Same goal, completely different approach!

quiz.q: What happens in explicit programming when the computer encounters a situation no rule was written for?
options: It learns from the situation automatically
It asks the user what to do
It fails or gives a wrong result — it can only follow written rules
It searches the internet for an answer
quiz.explanation: Explicit programming is limited — if no rule covers a situation, the program is stuck. ML handles new situations better because it generalises from patterns.

quiz.q: In Supervised Learning, what does 'labelled data' mean?
options: Data stored in labelled folders on a computer
Training examples where each input has a correct answer attached
Data that has been compressed and zipped
Information shared only with labelled users
quiz.explanation: Labelled data = examples with answers! Photo of a cat labelled 'CAT', photo of a dog labelled 'DOG'. The model learns by studying these labelled pairs.

quiz.q: Which tool lets students train their own image classifier in a browser without any code?
options: Microsoft Word
Wokwi Arduino Simulator
Google Teachable Machine
Scratch 3.0
quiz.explanation: Google Teachable Machine lets you train a real ML model using your webcam — no code needed! You can classify images, sounds, or poses.

quiz.q: Which is correct about AI, ML, and Deep Learning?
options: They are three completely different, unrelated technologies
AI is the biggest idea; ML is one way to achieve AI; Deep Learning is a powerful type of ML
Deep Learning came before Machine Learning
Machine Learning and AI mean exactly the same thing
quiz.explanation: Think of it as nested circles: AI (biggest) → Machine Learning (a type of AI) → Deep Learning (a powerful type of ML using neural networks).

options: When an AI runs too slowly
When the AI prefers a certain programming language
When an AI makes unfair decisions because its training data was unfair or incomplete
When the AI uses too much electricity
quiz.explanation: If training data is biased (e.g. only shows one type of face), the AI learns that bias and makes unfair predictions. Garbage in = garbage out!

quiz.q: Quick, Draw! by Google is an example of:
options: Explicit programming with drawing rules
A neural network that learned to recognise doodles from millions of examples
A simple calculator for artists
A robot that draws pictures
quiz.explanation: Quick, Draw! uses a neural network trained on millions of doodles from around the world. It guesses what you're drawing — that's ML in action!

quiz.q: A YouTube recommendation system that suggests videos you will enjoy is an example of:
options: Explicit programming — a human curates the list
Machine Learning — it learns your taste from your watch history
Random selection from all available videos
A timer-based system
quiz.explanation: YouTube's recommendation engine uses ML — it studies your watch history, likes, and search patterns to predict which videos you'll enjoy next!

quiz.q: In the card-sorting classroom activity, Round 2 (no rules given — learn from examples) represents:
options: Explicit programming
Hardware programming
Machine Learning
Binary coding
quiz.explanation: In Round 2, students received labelled examples and figured out patterns themselves — just like Machine Learning! No pre-written rules, just learning from data.
